title: CT Head — Basic Interpretation
oneLiner: CT head is the rapid, first-line imaging for acute neurological emergencies—trauma, stroke, altered consciousness—to rule out surgical lesions before it’s too late.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette (ward):** A 72-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents after a fall from standing height. Family says he was “a bit more drowsy” this morning, then vomited once. His GCS is 13 (E3 V4 M6), his right pupil is slightly sluggish, and he has mild left-sided weakness. He is on a warfarin (INR 3.5, last checked 2 weeks ago).  
*Dissect the vignette:*  
- **Age > 65 + anticoagulation:** high risk for traumatic intracranial hemorrhage despite minor mechanism. Subdural haematoma (SDH) is classic because bridging veins are fragile.  
- **Drowsiness → GCS 13:** mass effect from accumulating blood is compressing thalamus/reticular activating system.  
- **Sluggish right pupil:** early uncal herniation — the uncus pushes against CN III (ipsilateral first).  
- **Left-sided weakness:** contralateral corticospinal tract compression (Kernohan’s notch phenomenon can cause ipsilateral weakness later, but early is usually contralateral).  
- **Vomiting:** raised ICP → stimulation of area postrema.  

**Atypical presentations that trick you:**  
1. **“Headache only, no trauma”** — a 45-year-old with “worst headache of life” and normal neuro exam. Could be aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH). CT is 98% sensitive within 6 hours; after 12 hours drops to ~50% (then you need LP).  
2. **“Stroke mimic”** — a young woman on combined hormonal contraception with sudden hemiparesis. CT shows a hyperdense MCA sign (clot) but no early infarct—this is acute ischaemic stroke, but you must first exclude haemorrhage.  
3. **“Seizure + confusion”** — an 80-year-old with a 2-day history of confusion and one tonic-clonic seizure. CT shows a thin, crescentic hypodensity over the convexity — chronic subdural haematoma (CSDH). Often no clear trauma history.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** A CT head uses X-rays to create cross-sectional slices of the skull and brain. It distinguishes between high-density structures (bone, acute blood) and low-density structures (CSF, air, oedema, chronic blood). It’s the first test for anyone with new neurological deficit, head injury, or altered consciousness where “time is brain.”

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Every day in ED and on-call. You will interpret (or mis-interpret) dozens in your first year.  
- **Stakes:** Missing a subdural or epidural haematoma can lead to irreversible herniation. Missing a subarachnoid haemorrhage leaves a rebled risk of 4–30% in first 24 hours.  
- **Common pitfalls:** “Blood looks white”; but so does bone — you must window correctly. Thin SDHs along the falx can be invisible. Chronic blood becomes the same density as CSF and can be missed.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
Think of the CT head as a map of physical property: X-ray attenuation. Acute blood is dense because red cells are packed with protein (haemoglobin), giving a high attenuation number (50–80 HU). As blood ages, red cells lyse, protein breaks down → density falls → becomes isodense (a few days) then hypodense (weeks).

- **Subdural haematoma:** tearing of bridging veins (low-pressure) between dura and arachnoid. Blood spreads along the convexity in a crescentic shape, *crossing suture lines* but *not crossing dural reflections* (falx/tentorium).  
- **Epidural haematoma:** tearing of middle meningeal artery (high-pressure, arterial). Blood strips dura from skull, forming a biconvex (lentiform) lens that *does not cross suture lines*.  
- **Intraparenchymal haemorrhage:** hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small penetrating arteries (putamen, thalamus, pons, cerebellum). Acute hyperdense round/oval with surrounding hypodense oedema.  
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** aneurysm rupture → blood fills sulci, basal cisterns. CT shows hyperdensity in cisterns (e.g., perimesencephalic, sylvian fissure).  
- **Infarct:** cytotoxic oedema → reduced density (grey-white differentiation loss). Within 6 hours, subtle signs: sulcal effacement, insular ribbon sign, hyperdense artery.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
- The drowsy, anticoagulated elderly patient: bridging veins are fragile. Even a trivial fall can shear them → slow venous bleed → hours to days for symptoms.  
- The “worst headache” + normal exam: aneurysmal SAH. The blood irritates meninges → headache; no focal signs until vasospasm or rebleed.  
- The seizure + confusion older person: chronic SDH. Over weeks, blood liquefies → isodense then hypodense, but still mass effect → confusion, gait disturbance, seizures.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Key Finding | Classic Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Trauma** | SDH, EDH, contusion, SAH | Fall, RTC, assault; focal signs |
| **Spontaneous (Hypertensive)** | Intraparenchymal bleed (putamen, thalamus, pons) | Hypertensive patient with sudden hemiparesis + vomiting |
| **Aneurysmal** | SAH (basal cisterns) | “Worst headache of life” (thunderclap) |
| **Vascular malformation** | AVM bleed, cavernoma | Young adult, seizure + ICH, or incidental |
| **Anticoagulation** | Any haemorrhage, esp. SDH | High INR, recent fall, easy bruising |
| **Tumour** | Solitary/multiple hypodense/ hyperdense mass | Gradual onset headache, seizures, focal signs |
| **Infection** | Abscess, empyema | Fever, headache, focal signs; immunocompromised |
| **Ischaemic** | Infarct (territorial, watershed) | Sudden loss of function, atrial fibrillation, hyperdense artery |

**Most common cause overall:** trauma (falls in elderly). **Most common cause in young adult:** trauma (RTC, assault). **Most common non-traumatic cause:** hypertension (but in under-50s suspect vascular anomaly or mass).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:** None specific for CT head, but **ABCDE approach**:  
- **A – Airway/Bone** (not typical, but we use for fractures) — check bone windows for skull fracture.  
- **B – Blood** (hyperdense lesions) — acute haemorrhage.  
- **C – Cisterns** (open/effaced, hyperdense if SAH).  
- **D – Density (brain parenchyma)** — grey-white differentiation, symmetry.  
- **E – Everything else** (ventricles, midline shift, herniation).

**Single finding that confirms:**  
- **Acute haemorrhage:** hyperdensity (HU > 50) that doesn’t follow bone.  
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** hyperdensity in sylvian fissures, interhemispheric fissure, or suprasellar cistern.  
- **Epidural haematoma:** biconvex hyperdensity not crossing sutures.  
- **Subdural haematoma:** crescentic hyperdensity crossing sutures.  
- **Infarct (early):** loss of grey-white differentiation in a vascular territory, hyperdense vessel sign.

**What test to order:**  
- **CT head without contrast** for trauma, headache, stroke — that’s the standard.  
- **CT angiography** if SAH suspected (to find aneurysm) or if acute ischaemic stroke with large vessel occlusion.  
- **CT venography** if venous sinus thrombosis suspected (hyperdense sinus on non-contrast? Look for empty delta sign on contrast).  
- **MRI** is better for posterior fossa, small infarcts, but is second-line due to time.

**Pattern-recognition:**  
- A patient with GCS 13 and a crescentic hyperdensity over the left hemisphere → **acute SDH**. Confirm by checking if it crosses sutures (yes) and is not confined to a vascular territory.  
- A patient with “worst headache” and hyperdensity at the circle of Willis → **SAH**.  
- A patient with hypertension and sudden hemiparesis — round hyperdensity at putamen → **ICH**.  
- A trauma patient with a biconvex hyperdensity in temporal region → **EDH** — urgent neurosurgery.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Ensure ABC:** protect airway if GCS <8, control any bleeding (e.g., external haemorrhage).  
- **Reverse anticoagulation:** if on warfarin → **vitamin K 10 mg slow IV** + **prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 25–50 IU/kg IV** (or FFP if not available). For DOACs (apixaban/rivarelbant): **idarucizumab 5g IV** if dabigatran; **andexanet alfa** if factor Xa inhibitor.  
- **Lower raised ICP:** if signs of herniation (unequal pupils, decerebrate, Cushing’s reflex): **mannitol 1g/kg IV over 20 minutes** or **hypertonic saline 3% 250 ml bolus**, elevate head 30°, analgesia, sedation.  
- **Call neurosurgery immediately** if EDH, acute SDH with >5mm shift, or any acute haemorrhage with GCS <15.  
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** often given for cortical haemorrhage — **levetiracetam 500–1000 mg IV bd**.

**Pitfall:** Do NOT give aspirin or clopidogrel if you have a known or suspected haemorrhage. Do NOT delay CT to get labs — if the story fits, take them straight to scanner.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Neuro-obs every 15–30 minutes until stable** (GCS, pupils, limb power).  
- **Repeat CT:** if initial haemorrhage expands (especially in ICH — the “spot sign” on CT angiography predicts expansion).  
- **Blood pressure control:** for ICH, maintain systolic <140 mmHg if possible (e.g., labetalol, nicardipine).  
- **Seizure management:** if any seizure, start levetiracetam. Continue for 1 week if stopped.  
- **Nutrition:** early enteral feeding and speech and language therapy (SLT) assessment for swallow.  
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** cautious — low molecular weight heparin only after 24–48 hours without expansion (discuss with neurosurgery).  

**Long-term:**  
- **Rehabilitation:** physio, OT, SLT for deficits.  
- **Treat underlying cause:** aneurysmal clipping/coiling, antihypertensives, avoid anticoagulation if possible, or switch to safer agent.  
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** if no further seizures, no need for long-term antiepileptics after 1 week (except in certain high-risk ICH).  

**What NOT to do:**  
- Do not defer CT because of “stabilised” patient — subtle mass effect can herniate quickly.  
- Do not give mannitol without first confirming raised ICP or herniation (can cause hypotension).  
- Do not restart anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks post-ICH (discuss with haematology).  
- Do not delay LP for SAH if CT is negative but story is strong — do CT/LP within 12 hours of headache onset.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **Acute Subdural Haematoma** | **Epidural Haematoma** | **Subarachnoid Haemorrhage** | **Intraparenchymal Haemorrhage** |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Shape** | Crescentic | Biconvex (lentiform) | Fills sulci, cisterns | Round/oval |
| **Location** | Over convexity, crossing sutures | Temporal/parietal, not crossing sutures | Basal cisterns, sylvian fissures | Deep (putamen, thalamus, pons, cerebellum) |
| **Crosses sutures?** | Yes | No | N/A | N/A |
| **Crosses dural reflections?** | No (stays between dura-arachnoid) | Yes (stays between skull- dura) | N/A | N/A |
| **Key sign** | Chronic: isodense/hypodense; acute: hyperdense | Arterial bleed; lucent interval often absent | Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness | Hypertensive history, focal deficit |
| **Most common cause** | Trauma (venous bleed) | Trauma (arterial – MMA) | Aneurysm | Hypertension |
| **Urgent management** | Evacuation if >10mm midline shift, GCS <13 | Evacuation regardless of size | Secure aneurysm, nimodipine for vasospasm | BP control, reversal, consider surgery for posterior fossa |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Uncal herniation** | Ipsilateral fixed & dilated pupil, contralateral hemiparesis → then bilateral decerebrate posture, irregular respirations | CT shows midline shift >5mm, effacement of lateral ventricle | Immediate hyperventilation, mannitol, neurosurgical evacuation |
| **Hydrocephalus** | Worsening consciousness, headache, vomiting, abducens palsy | Enlarged temporal horns (>2mm), ballooned frontal horns, effacement of sulci | EVD (external ventricular drain) |
| **Rebleed after SAH** | Sudden deterioration, new severe headache | Repeat CT shows increased SAH | Secure aneurysm, urgently |
| **Vasospasm (SAH)** | New focal deficit days 4–14 post-SAH, confusion | CT angiography shows arterial narrowing | Triple-H therapy (hypertension, hypervolaemia, haemodilution), nimodipine 60mg 4-hourly PO |
| **Seizures** | Any altered event, tongue bite, incontinence | New spike-wave on EEG | Levetiracetam load, monitor |
| **Chronic SDH expansion** | Gradual confusion, gait disturbance in weeks | CT shows hypodense/isodense crescent with flattening of sulci | Burr hole evacuation if symptomatic |

**Late presentation pattern:** The patient who had a minor fall 3 weeks ago, now with “dementia-like” symptoms — may be a chronic SDH. Untreated, it can cause persistent cognitive decline or acute decompensation (seizure, herniation). Always think of it in elderly with any new confusion.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**  
- **“Blood Can Be Very Bad”** — Systematic CT head review:  
  - **B** — Blood (hyperdensity)  
  - **C** — Cisterns (open? hyperdense?)  
  - **B** — Brain (grey-white, symmetry)  
  - **V** — Ventricles (size, midline shift)  
  - **B** — Bone (fracture)  
- **“ICH – It Changed Huge”** for timing of acute blood:  
  - **I** — Intraparenchymal (first 6h hyperdense)  
  - **C** — Changes to isodense (days 2–5)  
  - **H** — Hypodense (weeks)  
- **“BATS”** for SDH vs EDH:  
  - **B** — Biconvex = Extradural  
  - **A** — Across sutures = Subdural  
  - **T** — Temporal = high risk for EDH (if middle meningeal artery)  
  - **S** — Sutures don’t cross = EDH  

**Clinical Pearls:**  
1. **Always window bone** — to see whether the hyperdensity is really blood or bone (e.g., inner table fracture can mimic haemorrhage).  
2. **The insular ribbon sign** — early MCA infarct: hypodensity of insular cortex relative to lentiform nucleus.  
3. **Hypodense sulci** — chronic SDH: the sulci on the convexity are compressed (effaced) on the side of the haematoma, but may not look “dense” if chronic.  
4. **“Empty delta sign”** on CT venography — central filling defect in superior sagittal sinus = venous sinus thrombosis.  
5. **Never say “normal CT head” after a SAH story** — if CT is negative but story is thunderclap, do the LP.  
6. **Cerebellar ICH** — watch for rapid deterioration; a small bleed can cause obstructive hydrocephalus.  
7. **Midline shift >5mm is dangerous**, but >10mm is usually a surgical emergency (if not herniated).  

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**  
- Mistaking a calcified pineal gland or choroid plexus for a haemorrhage (they are symmetric and have high density but are small and lay in normal location).  
- Missing a thin convexity SDH because you didn’t look at the top slices.  
- Thinking a chronic SDH is normal atrophy — check for effacement of sulci and a subtle hypodense layer.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to escalate | What to say | Thresholds |
|------------------|-------------|------------|
| **GCS drop by 2+ points** | “Patient deteriorating neurologically, needs immediate CT and neurosurgery review” | GCS < 15 in anyone with known intracranial pathology |
| **New or worsening focal signs** | “Right-sided weakness now complete, possible herniation” | |
| **Unequal pupils (new)** | “Uncal herniation suspected” | |
| **Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)** | “Raised ICP with imminent herniation” | |
| **Seizure >5 minutes or multiple** | “Status epilepticus, needs anaesthetic review” | |
| **New hypotension or hypoxia** | “Suggests other organ failure or sepsis” | NEWS2 >6 |
| **Rising lactate (>4 mmol/L)** | “May be sepsis or mesenteric ischaemia” | Septic screen |
| **INR >4.5 (on warfarin) with ICH** | “Needs PCC urgently” | |
| **Any acute surgical haemorrhage (EDH, SDH >10mm shift, posterior fossa bleed)** | “Needs immediate neurosurgery” | GCS <=13, midline shift >5mm |

**Bottom line for juniors:** If the CT shows any acute intracranial blood or mass effect, call the neurosurgery registrar *now*. Don’t wait for obs to deteriorate. Your pattern-recognition from the vignette will save lives.

## CT Head — Basic Interpretation
**One-liner:** CT head is the rapid, first-line imaging for acute neurological emergencies—trauma, stroke, altered consciousness—to rule out surgical lesions before it’s too late.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic vignette (ward):** A 72-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents after a fall from standing height. Family says he was “a bit more drowsy” this morning, then vomited once. His GCS is 13 (E3 V4 M6), his right pupil is slightly sluggish, and he has mild left-sided weakness. He is on a warfarin (INR 3.5, last checked 2 weeks ago).  
*Dissect the vignette:*  
- **Age > 65 + anticoagulation:** high risk for traumatic intracranial hemorrhage despite minor mechanism. Subdural haematoma (SDH) is classic because bridging veins are fragile.  
- **Drowsiness → GCS 13:** mass effect from accumulating blood is compressing thalamus/reticular activating system.  
- **Sluggish right pupil:** early uncal herniation — the uncus pushes against CN III (ipsilateral first).  
- **Left-sided weakness:** contralateral corticospinal tract compression (Kernohan’s notch phenomenon can cause ipsilateral weakness later, but early is usually contralateral).  
- **Vomiting:** raised ICP → stimulation of area postrema.  

**Atypical presentations that trick you:**  
1. **“Headache only, no trauma”** — a 45-year-old with “worst headache of life” and normal neuro exam. Could be aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH). CT is 98% sensitive within 6 hours; after 12 hours drops to ~50% (then you need LP).  
2. **“Stroke mimic”** — a young woman on combined hormonal contraception with sudden hemiparesis. CT shows a hyperdense MCA sign (clot) but no early infarct—this is acute ischaemic stroke, but you must first exclude haemorrhage.  
3. **“Seizure + confusion”** — an 80-year-old with a 2-day history of confusion and one tonic-clonic seizure. CT shows a thin, crescentic hypodensity over the convexity — chronic subdural haematoma (CSDH). Often no clear trauma history.  

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** A CT head uses X-rays to create cross-sectional slices of the skull and brain. It distinguishes between high-density structures (bone, acute blood) and low-density structures (CSF, air, oedema, chronic blood). It’s the first test for anyone with new neurological deficit, head injury, or altered consciousness where “time is brain.”

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Every day in ED and on-call. You will interpret (or mis-interpret) dozens in your first year.  
- **Stakes:** Missing a subdural or epidural haematoma can lead to irreversible herniation. Missing a subarachnoid haemorrhage leaves a rebled risk of 4–30% in first 24 hours.  
- **Common pitfalls:** “Blood looks white”; but so does bone — you must window correctly. Thin SDHs along the falx can be invisible. Chronic blood becomes the same density as CSF and can be missed.  

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

Think of the CT head as a map of physical property: X-ray attenuation. Acute blood is dense because red cells are packed with protein (haemoglobin), giving a high attenuation number (50–80 HU). As blood ages, red cells lyse, protein breaks down → density falls → becomes isodense (a few days) then hypodense (weeks).

- **Subdural haematoma:** tearing of bridging veins (low-pressure) between dura and arachnoid. Blood spreads along the convexity in a crescentic shape, *crossing suture lines* but *not crossing dural reflections* (falx/tentorium).  
- **Epidural haematoma:** tearing of middle meningeal artery (high-pressure, arterial). Blood strips dura from skull, forming a biconvex (lentiform) lens that *does not cross suture lines*.  
- **Intraparenchymal haemorrhage:** hypertension causes lipohyalinosis of small penetrating arteries (putamen, thalamus, pons, cerebellum). Acute hyperdense round/oval with surrounding hypodense oedema.  
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** aneurysm rupture → blood fills sulci, basal cisterns. CT shows hyperdensity in cisterns (e.g., perimesencephalic, sylvian fissure).  
- **Infarct:** cytotoxic oedema → reduced density (grey-white differentiation loss). Within 6 hours, subtle signs: sulcal effacement, insular ribbon sign, hyperdense artery.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**  
- The drowsy, anticoagulated elderly patient: bridging veins are fragile. Even a trivial fall can shear them → slow venous bleed → hours to days for symptoms.  
- The “worst headache” + normal exam: aneurysmal SAH. The blood irritates meninges → headache; no focal signs until vasospasm or rebleed.  
- The seizure + confusion older person: chronic SDH. Over weeks, blood liquefies → isodense then hypodense, but still mass effect → confusion, gait disturbance, seizures.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Key Finding | Classic Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Trauma** | SDH, EDH, contusion, SAH | Fall, RTC, assault; focal signs |
| **Spontaneous (Hypertensive)** | Intraparenchymal bleed (putamen, thalamus, pons) | Hypertensive patient with sudden hemiparesis + vomiting |
| **Aneurysmal** | SAH (basal cisterns) | “Worst headache of life” (thunderclap) |
| **Vascular malformation** | AVM bleed, cavernoma | Young adult, seizure + ICH, or incidental |
| **Anticoagulation** | Any haemorrhage, esp. SDH | High INR, recent fall, easy bruising |
| **Tumour** | Solitary/multiple hypodense/ hyperdense mass | Gradual onset headache, seizures, focal signs |
| **Infection** | Abscess, empyema | Fever, headache, focal signs; immunocompromised |
| **Ischaemic** | Infarct (territorial, watershed) | Sudden loss of function, atrial fibrillation, hyperdense artery |

**Most common cause overall:** trauma (falls in elderly). **Most common cause in young adult:** trauma (RTC, assault). **Most common non-traumatic cause:** hypertension (but in under-50s suspect vascular anomaly or mass).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring systems:** None specific for CT head, but **ABCDE approach**:  
- **A – Airway/Bone** (not typical, but we use for fractures) — check bone windows for skull fracture.  
- **B – Blood** (hyperdense lesions) — acute haemorrhage.  
- **C – Cisterns** (open/effaced, hyperdense if SAH).  
- **D – Density (brain parenchyma)** — grey-white differentiation, symmetry.  
- **E – Everything else** (ventricles, midline shift, herniation).

**Single finding that confirms:**  
- **Acute haemorrhage:** hyperdensity (HU > 50) that doesn’t follow bone.  
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** hyperdensity in sylvian fissures, interhemispheric fissure, or suprasellar cistern.  
- **Epidural haematoma:** biconvex hyperdensity not crossing sutures.  
- **Subdural haematoma:** crescentic hyperdensity crossing sutures.  
- **Infarct (early):** loss of grey-white differentiation in a vascular territory, hyperdense vessel sign.

**What test to order:**  
- **CT head without contrast** for trauma, headache, stroke — that’s the standard.  
- **CT angiography** if SAH suspected (to find aneurysm) or if acute ischaemic stroke with large vessel occlusion.  
- **CT venography** if venous sinus thrombosis suspected (hyperdense sinus on non-contrast? Look for empty delta sign on contrast).  
- **MRI** is better for posterior fossa, small infarcts, but is second-line due to time.

**Pattern-recognition:**  
- A patient with GCS 13 and a crescentic hyperdensity over the left hemisphere → **acute SDH**. Confirm by checking if it crosses sutures (yes) and is not confined to a vascular territory.  
- A patient with “worst headache” and hyperdensity at the circle of Willis → **SAH**.  
- A patient with hypertension and sudden hemiparesis — round hyperdensity at putamen → **ICH**.  
- A trauma patient with a biconvex hyperdensity in temporal region → **EDH** — urgent neurosurgery.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Ensure ABC:** protect airway if GCS <8, control any bleeding (e.g., external haemorrhage).  
- **Reverse anticoagulation:** if on warfarin → **vitamin K 10 mg slow IV** + **prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 25–50 IU/kg IV** (or FFP if not available). For DOACs (apixaban/rivarelbant): **idarucizumab 5g IV** if dabigatran; **andexanet alfa** if factor Xa inhibitor.  
- **Lower raised ICP:** if signs of herniation (unequal pupils, decerebrate, Cushing’s reflex): **mannitol 1g/kg IV over 20 minutes** or **hypertonic saline 3% 250 ml bolus**, elevate head 30°, analgesia, sedation.  
- **Call neurosurgery immediately** if EDH, acute SDH with >5mm shift, or any acute haemorrhage with GCS <15.  
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** often given for cortical haemorrhage — **levetiracetam 500–1000 mg IV bd**.

**Pitfall:** Do NOT give aspirin or clopidogrel if you have a known or suspected haemorrhage. Do NOT delay CT to get labs — if the story fits, take them straight to scanner.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
- **Neuro-obs every 15–30 minutes until stable** (GCS, pupils, limb power).  
- **Repeat CT:** if initial haemorrhage expands (especially in ICH — the “spot sign” on CT angiography predicts expansion).  
- **Blood pressure control:** for ICH, maintain systolic <140 mmHg if possible (e.g., labetalol, nicardipine).  
- **Seizure management:** if any seizure, start levetiracetam. Continue for 1 week if stopped.  
- **Nutrition:** early enteral feeding and speech and language therapy (SLT) assessment for swallow.  
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** cautious — low molecular weight heparin only after 24–48 hours without expansion (discuss with neurosurgery).  

**Long-term:**  
- **Rehabilitation:** physio, OT, SLT for deficits.  
- **Treat underlying cause:** aneurysmal clipping/coiling, antihypertensives, avoid anticoagulation if possible, or switch to safer agent.  
- **Seizure prophylaxis:** if no further seizures, no need for long-term antiepileptics after 1 week (except in certain high-risk ICH).  

**What NOT to do:**  
- Do not defer CT because of “stabilised” patient — subtle mass effect can herniate quickly.  
- Do not give mannitol without first confirming raised ICP or herniation (can cause hypotension).  
- Do not restart anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks post-ICH (discuss with haematology).  
- Do not delay LP for SAH if CT is negative but story is strong — do CT/LP within 12 hours of headache onset.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | **Acute Subdural Haematoma** | **Epidural Haematoma** | **Subarachnoid Haemorrhage** | **Intraparenchymal Haemorrhage** |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Shape** | Crescentic | Biconvex (lentiform) | Fills sulci, cisterns | Round/oval |
| **Location** | Over convexity, crossing sutures | Temporal/parietal, not crossing sutures | Basal cisterns, sylvian fissures | Deep (putamen, thalamus, pons, cerebellum) |
| **Crosses sutures?** | Yes | No | N/A | N/A |
| **Crosses dural reflections?** | No (stays between dura-arachnoid) | Yes (stays between skull- dura) | N/A | N/A |
| **Key sign** | Chronic: isodense/hypodense; acute: hyperdense | Arterial bleed; lucent interval often absent | Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness | Hypertensive history, focal deficit |
| **Most common cause** | Trauma (venous bleed) | Trauma (arterial – MMA) | Aneurysm | Hypertension |
| **Urgent management** | Evacuation if >10mm midline shift, GCS <13 | Evacuation regardless of size | Secure aneurysm, nimodipine for vasospasm | BP control, reversal, consider surgery for posterior fossa |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Uncal herniation** | Ipsilateral fixed & dilated pupil, contralateral hemiparesis → then bilateral decerebrate posture, irregular respirations | CT shows midline shift >5mm, effacement of lateral ventricle | Immediate hyperventilation, mannitol, neurosurgical evacuation |
| **Hydrocephalus** | Worsening consciousness, headache, vomiting, abducens palsy | Enlarged temporal horns (>2mm), ballooned frontal horns, effacement of sulci | EVD (external ventricular drain) |
| **Rebleed after SAH** | Sudden deterioration, new severe headache | Repeat CT shows increased SAH | Secure aneurysm, urgently |
| **Vasospasm (SAH)** | New focal deficit days 4–14 post-SAH, confusion | CT angiography shows arterial narrowing | Triple-H therapy (hypertension, hypervolaemia, haemodilution), nimodipine 60mg 4-hourly PO |
| **Seizures** | Any altered event, tongue bite, incontinence | New spike-wave on EEG | Levetiracetam load, monitor |
| **Chronic SDH expansion** | Gradual confusion, gait disturbance in weeks | CT shows hypodense/isodense crescent with flattening of sulci | Burr hole evacuation if symptomatic |

**Late presentation pattern:** The patient who had a minor fall 3 weeks ago, now with “dementia-like” symptoms — may be a chronic SDH. Untreated, it can cause persistent cognitive decline or acute decompensation (seizure, herniation). Always think of it in elderly with any new confusion.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**  
- **“Blood Can Be Very Bad”** — Systematic CT head review:  
  - **B** — Blood (hyperdensity)  
  - **C** — Cisterns (open? hyperdense?)  
  - **B** — Brain (grey-white, symmetry)  
  - **V** — Ventricles (size, midline shift)  
  - **B** — Bone (fracture)  
- **“ICH – It Changed Huge”** for timing of acute blood:  
  - **I** — Intraparenchymal (first 6h hyperdense)  
  - **C** — Changes to isodense (days 2–5)  
  - **H** — Hypodense (weeks)  
- **“BATS”** for SDH vs EDH:  
  - **B** — Biconvex = Extradural  
  - **A** — Across sutures = Subdural  
  - **T** — Temporal = high risk for EDH (if middle meningeal artery)  
  - **S** — Sutures don’t cross = EDH  

**Clinical Pearls:**  
1. **Always window bone** — to see whether the hyperdensity is really blood or bone (e.g., inner table fracture can mimic haemorrhage).  
2. **The insular ribbon sign** — early MCA infarct: hypodensity of insular cortex relative to lentiform nucleus.  
3. **Hypodense sulci** — chronic SDH: the sulci on the convexity are compressed (effaced) on the side of the haematoma, but may not look “dense” if chronic.  
4. **“Empty delta sign”** on CT venography — central filling defect in superior sagittal sinus = venous sinus thrombosis.  
5. **Never say “normal CT head” after a SAH story** — if CT is negative but story is thunderclap, do the LP.  
6. **Cerebellar ICH** — watch for rapid deterioration; a small bleed can cause obstructive hydrocephalus.  
7. **Midline shift >5mm is dangerous**, but >10mm is usually a surgical emergency (if not herniated).  

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**  
- Mistaking a calcified pineal gland or choroid plexus for a haemorrhage (they are symmetric and have high density but are small and lay in normal location).  
- Missing a thin convexity SDH because you didn’t look at the top slices.  
- Thinking a chronic SDH is normal atrophy — check for effacement of sulci and a subtle hypodense layer.  

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to escalate | What to say | Thresholds |
|------------------|-------------|------------|
| **GCS drop by 2+ points** | “Patient deteriorating neurologically, needs immediate CT and neurosurgery review” | GCS < 15 in anyone with known intracranial pathology |
| **New or worsening focal signs** | “Right-sided weakness now complete, possible herniation” | |
| **Unequal pupils (new)** | “Uncal herniation suspected” | |
| **Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)** | “Raised ICP with imminent herniation” | |
| **Seizure >5 minutes or multiple** | “Status epilepticus, needs anaesthetic review” | |
| **New hypotension or hypoxia** | “Suggests other organ failure or sepsis” | NEWS2 >6 |
| **Rising lactate (>4 mmol/L)** | “May be sepsis or mesenteric ischaemia” | Septic screen |
| **INR >4.5 (on warfarin) with ICH** | “Needs PCC urgently” | |
| **Any acute surgical haemorrhage (EDH, SDH >10mm shift, posterior fossa bleed)** | “Needs immediate neurosurgery” | GCS <=13, midline shift >5mm |

**Bottom line for juniors:** If the CT shows any acute intracranial blood or mass effect, call the neurosurgery registrar *now*. Don’t wait for obs to deteriorate. Your pattern-recognition from the vignette will save lives.
